context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on Problem Set 4 for vibrations and waves, and I’m most stuck on the beaded string normal modes in 4.1. I think the wall boundary conditions are the confusing part for me, especially why something like \(A_1=-A_0\) shows up.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Let's build up to it. Think about the physical setup: the left wall is fixed, and the nearest bead is a distance a/2 from it. What does 'fixed wall' mean in terms of the displacement at the wall's location?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 4.1(a). Rather than explaining the boundary condition, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to discover why A1=-A0 arises. The key insight is that a fixed wall enforces zero displacement at the wall's position. I'll ask them to think about what the wall actually does physically — what constraint does it impose — before introducing the infinite system idea.

exchanges.student: I think it means the displacement at the wall itself has to be zero at all times. I’m just not fully seeing how that turns into a condition on these bead amplitudes instead of directly on \(y\) at the wall.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, in the 'infinite system' trick, imagine placing a ghost bead (bead 0) on the other side of the wall, at the same distance a/2 from the wall as bead 1 is. The wall sits exactly midway between bead 0 and bead 1. Given that the displacement must be zero at the wall, what does that tell you about the relationship between the displacements of bead 0 and bead 1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the zero-displacement condition at the wall. Now I need to guide them toward connecting that physical condition to the bead amplitudes. The key step is recognizing that in the infinite system trick, we place an imaginary bead (bead 0) on the other side of the wall, symmetrically. The wall sits exactly halfway between bead 0 and bead 1. If the displacement must be zero at the wall's position, and the wall is midway between beads 0 and 1, what does that imply about A0 and A1? I'll ask them to think about where exactly the wall sits relative to the imaginary bead.

exchanges.student: Oh, I think since the wall is halfway between them and the displacement there has to be zero, the midpoint displacement should vanish. So that would mean \(y_0\) and \(y_1\) have to be equal and opposite, right? Then for a normal mode I guess that becomes \(A_0=-A_1\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — you've got it. That's precisely the reasoning behind A1 = −A0. Now, can you apply the same logic to the right-hand wall to find the analogous boundary condition for part (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived A0 = -A1, which is equivalent to A1 = -A0 as stated in the problem. This is good progress. I should affirm this and nudge them toward part (b) — the right-hand wall boundary condition — which follows analogous reasoning but with a subtle difference in geometry (the last bead is also a/2 from the right wall, so they should be able to apply the same logic independently).

exchanges.student: I think it should be the same idea on the other side: if the ghost bead past the right wall is labeled \(N+1\), then the wall is midway between beads \(N\) and \(N+1\), so their displacements should also be opposite. So I get \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\).
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's correct. Now for part (c): given these two boundary conditions, what form would you propose for the displacement of the n-th bead in a normal mode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the right-wall boundary condition. Both boundary conditions are now in hand. Time to move to part (c) — finding the normal modes and frequencies. I should prompt them to think about what form the normal mode solution takes for a beaded string, connecting to what they know about traveling waves or standing waves on a discrete system.

exchanges.student: I’d guess something like \(A_n = C e^{ikna} + D e^{-ikna}\), since for the infinite beaded string the mode shapes should be exponential or sinusoidal in \(n\). I’m not sure yet whether it’s better to switch to sines/cosines right away.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Try applying your left-wall boundary condition A1 = −A0 directly to that form. What does that tell you about the relationship between C and D?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a correct general form. I should nudge them to apply the boundary condition A1 = -A0 to their proposed form to see what constraint emerges, rather than just telling them to switch to sines/cosines. This will naturally lead them to discover the sine form and the quantization of k.

exchanges.student:
Let me try it.  
\(A_0 = C + D\) and \(A_1 = Ce^{ika} + De^{-ika}\).  
Then \(A_1=-A_0\) gives
\[
Ce^{ika}+De^{-ika}=-(C+D).
\]
I’m not immediately seeing the clean relation between \(C\) and \(D\), unless it simplifies better in sine form.
exchanges.tutor: Hint: try assuming C = −D and check whether that satisfies your equation. What does the mode shape A_n look like in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but struggling to see the simplification. Rather than giving the answer, I'll give a small nudge: try setting C = -D and see if that satisfies the equation. This will lead them to discover the sine form naturally.

exchanges.student:
If \(C=-D\), then
\[
A_n = C(e^{ikna}-e^{-ikna}) = 2iC\sin(kna),
\]
so it’s basically a sine mode. But now I’m a little confused, because then \(A_0=0\), and I thought the ghost bead wasn’t literally fixed at zero displacement.
exchanges.tutor: Good derivation! Think about it this way: A0 = 0 is perfectly consistent with A1 = −A0, since both sides equal zero... but is A0 = 0 actually required by the boundary condition, or is it just a consequence of the sine form you found? What does the boundary condition really fix?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the sine form but is confused about A0=0. This is a good conceptual question worth addressing briefly. A0=0 is actually consistent: the ghost bead is defined precisely so that the midpoint (wall) has zero displacement, and with A_n = sin(kna), A0 = sin(0) = 0, which is consistent. The ghost bead isn't physically there — it's a mathematical device. I should ask them to reconcile this themselves rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: I think the boundary condition only fixes a relation between neighboring amplitudes across the wall, not that either one has to be zero by itself. So \(A_0=0\) would just be a special consequence of choosing pure \(\sin(kna)\), not the most general requirement. Could you give me a clearer hint for the better form to use here?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, the sine form is correct — bead 0 is just a ghost, so A0 = 0 is perfectly fine. Don't get too hung up on this. Now move forward: apply your right-wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = −A_N to A_n = sin(kna). What condition does that give you on k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overthinking this. The sine form A_n = sin(kna) IS the correct form here — A0=0 is fine because bead 0 is the ghost bead, not a real bead. The real beads are 1 through N. I should redirect them to now apply the RIGHT-wall boundary condition to quantize k, rather than dwelling on this point.

exchanges.student:
Okay, using \(A_n=\sin(kna)\),

\[
\sin(k(N+1)a)=-\sin(kNa).
\]

Then
\[
\sin(k(N+1)a)+\sin(kNa)=0.
\]

Using the sum identity, I think that gives
\[
2\sin\!\left(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{ka}{2}\right)=0.
\]

So maybe the allowed \(k\) values come from  
\[
\sin\!\left(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\right)=0
\]
or possibly \(\cos(ka/2)=0\)? I’m not sure which branch is the physical one.
exchanges.tutor: Good algebra. Now think about physics: a system of N beads should have exactly N normal modes. How many distinct, non-trivial k values does each branch give you? That should help you figure out which branch (or both) contributes.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the sum-to-product identity correctly and identified two branches. I should ask them to think about which branch gives physically distinct, non-trivial modes. The cos(ka/2)=0 branch gives k = π/a, 3π/a, etc. The sin branch gives k = mπ/((N+1/2)a) = 2mπ/(2N+1)a. Both are potentially valid — I should ask them to think about how many independent normal modes a system of N beads should have, and which branch(es) give that count.

exchanges.student: I think the \(\sin\!\left(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\right)=0\) branch gives a whole set of discrete \(k\)'s, while \(\cos(ka/2)=0\) seems like it might just give special cases like \(ka=\pi\). I’m guessing I should keep the branch that gives exactly \(N\) nontrivial modes, but I’m not fully sure how to count them cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking. For the sin branch, write out the explicit k values: if sin((2N+1)ka/2)=0, then (2N+1)ka/2 = mπ for integer m. List the first several m values and check: which ones give a non-trivial sine mode (i.e., A_n not identically zero for all beads)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but needs to count more carefully. I should ask them to write out the explicit k values from the sin branch and count how many give distinct, non-trivial sine modes (i.e., not sin=0 identically). This will lead them to the answer m=1,...,N. I should keep this bite-sized.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student step-by-step through the beaded string boundary condition problem without giving away answers. Each tutor turn built on the student's prior response, acknowledged correct reasoning, and posed a focused follow-up question. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting of student work occurred, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to Problem 4.1. Responses were concise and appropriately sized for the complexity of each step.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1: the tutor never provided final answers or near-submission-ready reasoning; all key derivations (C=−D, sine form, quantization condition) were elicited from the student. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps and built directly on them without restarting or ignoring progress. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level guidance (e.g., 'try assuming C=−D and check,' 'think about physics: N beads should have N modes') rather than just correctness judgments. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling occurred, and the conversation advanced steadily through the problem. Communication was bite-sized and clear, with each tutor turn posing one focused question. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
